specification.topic-statement: The error result system defines a typed, stack-allocated result type that carries either a success value or a heap-allocated error descriptor, together with a fixed set of error codes and macros for constructing, propagating, and inspecting results.

scope.in-scope: The result type and its two variants (success and failure), the error descriptor structure and its fields, the complete set of named error codes, error construction with formatted messages, result checking predicates, error-code-to-string conversion, error message retrieval with fallback, formatted diagnostic output, early-return propagation macros, the injectable allocator used to create error descriptors, and the PANIC invocation on allocation failure or invalid code.
scope.boundary: When allocation fails during error construction, or when an unrecognized error code is passed to the code-to-string converter, the system invokes the process-terminating panic sequence defined elsewhere. The details of that sequence — terminal restoration, log write, stderr output, abort — are outside this specification.

Error descriptors are heap-allocated under a caller-supplied parent context using hierarchical memory management. Ownership, lifetime, and deallocation semantics are governed by the memory management layer. This specification records only that descriptors are allocated on the supplied parent context and that allocation failures trigger panic rather than returning a secondary error.

The allocator used to create error descriptors is a separately defined function with a weak-symbol declaration, allowing tests to substitute a failing allocator to exercise the allocation-failure panic path. The substitution mechanism itself is outside this specification.

data-contracts.contract: A fixed enumeration of twelve named codes. The success sentinel has numeric value zero. All failure codes have positive values.
A heap-allocated structure carrying a classified, located, human-readable description of a single failure. Always allocated under a caller-supplied parent context.
A stack-allocated discriminated union. Carries either a non-typed success pointer or a pointer to an error descriptor. The discriminant is separate from the union.

behavior.result-construction-success: A success result is constructed by supplying a void pointer value. The resulting result has its discriminant set to false and its success field set to the supplied pointer. The pointer may be NULL; no assertion or check is performed on its value. Construction is always inlined at the call site.
behavior.result-construction-failure: A failure result is constructed by supplying a non-NULL error descriptor pointer. If the pointer is NULL the process panics immediately via an assertion. The resulting result has its discriminant set to true and its error field set to the supplied pointer. Construction is always inlined at the call site.
behavior.error-descriptor-construction:
An error descriptor is constructed by supplying: a parent memory context, an error code, a source file name string, a source line number, a printf-style format string, and any format arguments.
The descriptor is allocated as a zero-initialized block of exactly the size of the descriptor structure, under the supplied parent context. If allocation fails, the process panics immediately; no error is returned.
The code, file pointer, and line number are stored directly. The format string and arguments are rendered into the 256-byte message buffer using bounded formatted string printing. If the rendered output would exceed 255 characters, it is silently truncated and null-terminated.
If the formatted printing function itself reports a failure (returning a negative value), the process panics immediately.
Errors encountered while constructing an error descriptor do not produce a secondary error result; they terminate the process immediately. There is no graceful fallback path.
The branch that panics on a negative return from the formatted printing function is explicitly excluded from coverage measurement. The comment in the source acknowledges that exercising this path would require a circular dependency — mocking the print function in error-construction code that is itself part of the error reporting infrastructure.
behavior.public-construction-macros:
Two macros form the intended public API for creating results.
The success macro wraps the construction call with no additional behavior. It accepts a single pointer value and produces a success result.
The failure macro combines error descriptor construction with result wrapping. It accepts: a parent context, a bare suffix name (which is prefixed to form the full error code), a format string, and optional format arguments. It automatically captures the current source file name and line number, so the descriptor always reflects the call site of the macro, not any internal helper.
The failure macro concatenates a fixed prefix with the provided suffix to form the code name. Passing an unrecognized suffix is a compile-time error, not a runtime failure.
behavior.result-checking-predicates: Two predicates inspect a result. Both accept a non-NULL pointer to a result; passing NULL causes an assertion failure. The success predicate returns true when the discriminant is false. The failure predicate returns true when the discriminant is true. Both are always inlined.
behavior.error-propagation-macros:
Two macros provide automatic early-return propagation when a called expression produces a failure result.
The first (CHECK) evaluates the expression, captures the result in a local variable, and if the discriminant is true returns that result immediately from the enclosing function. No value is extracted from a success result; the macro is used for side-effecting calls where the success payload is irrelevant.
The second (TRY) evaluates the expression, captures the result, and if the discriminant is true returns that result immediately. On success it yields the success pointer as the value of the macro expression, allowing direct assignment. This macro uses a compiler statement expression and requires a compiler that supports that extension.
Both macros use a do-while(0) or statement-expression wrapper to ensure correct behavior when placed inside conditional branches without braces.
behavior.error-code-to-string-conversion:
A predicate maps each of the twelve error codes to a fixed human-readable English string: - OK -> "OK" - INVALID_ARG -> "Invalid argument" - OUT_OF_RANGE -> "Out of range" - IO -> "IO error" - PARSE -> "Parse error" - DB_CONNECT -> "Database connection error" - DB_MIGRATE -> "Database migration error" - OUT_OF_MEMORY -> "Out of memory" - AGENT_NOT_FOUND -> "Agent not found" - PROVIDER -> "Provider error" - MISSING_CREDENTIALS -> "Missing credentials" - NOT_IMPLEMENTED -> "Not implemented"
If a numeric value outside the enumeration is supplied, the process panics immediately. The switch default arm is excluded from coverage measurement because valid code never reaches it.
The success sentinel code (value 0) is a valid input and maps to the string "OK". The function does not distinguish between success and failure codes; it converts any recognized code, including the success code.
behavior.error-message-retrieval:
Given a non-NULL error descriptor pointer, the human-readable message is obtained as follows: if the message buffer's first byte is non-zero (i.e., a non-empty formatted message was stored at construction time), the buffer pointer is returned directly. Otherwise the error code is converted to its canonical string using the code-to-string converter, and that string is returned.
A descriptor constructed with an empty format string or a format that renders to an empty string silently falls back to the canonical code description. The empty-message condition is undetectable by the caller.
behavior.error-inspection: Given a non-NULL error descriptor pointer, the stored error code is returned directly. Passing NULL causes an assertion failure.
behavior.formatted-diagnostic-output:
Given a non-NULL output stream and a non-NULL error descriptor pointer, a single line is written to the stream using the standard formatted output function. The format is:
Error: <message> [<file>:<line>]\n
The message is obtained via the message-retrieval behavior above. The file field is the literal pointer stored in the descriptor; if that pointer is NULL, the string "unknown" is substituted. The line number is formatted as a signed decimal 32-bit integer. Passing NULL for either the stream or the descriptor causes an assertion failure.
behavior.injectable-allocator:
Error descriptor allocation is performed through a separately defined function rather than by calling the memory manager directly. That function accepts a parent context and a byte count, asserts the context is non-NULL, and delegates to the underlying zero-initializing allocator.
Because the function is separately defined (not inlined), test code can substitute a replacement implementation that simulates allocation failure. This allows the allocation-failure panic path inside error descriptor construction to be exercised under test.
The substitution mechanism relies on a linkage attribute that allows a test-provided definition to override the production definition at link time, rather than a runtime function pointer. This means the injection is binary-level, not configurable at runtime.
